verses.text: And Benhadad the king of Syria gathered all his host together: and there were thirty and two kings with him, and horses, and chariots: and he went up and besieged Samaria, and warred against it. And he sent messengers to Ahab king of Israel into the city, and said unto him, Thus saith Benhadad, Thy silver and thy gold is mine; thy wives also and thy children, even the goodliest, are mine. And the king of Israel answered and said, My lord, O king, according to thy saying, I am thine, and all that I have. Guidance: Ben-hadad's siege. Ben-hadad II of Aram (Damascus) — son of the Ben-hadad I to whom Asa appealed in 1 Kgs 15 — has now reached the peak of his regional power. He gathers his army with 32 vassal-kings as allies, marches across the territory of Israel, and besieges Samaria itself. The opening demand is extortionate: 'Thy silver and thy gold is mine; thy wives also and thy children, even the goodliest, are mine.' Hebrew naphshekha li ve-tovim li — your good things are mine. He is claiming total ownership of Ahab's wealth and royal family. Ahab's response is shocking. He surrenders unconditionally: 'My lord, O king, according to thy saying, I am thine, and all that I have.' The Israelite king has accepted vassalage. He calls the Aramean king 'my lord' (adoni), recognizes Ben-hadad's claim to his property, even concedes his wives and children. The chapter is showing us Ahab as a politically weak and spiritually empty king. He has no theological resources to draw on. He is being besieged by a foreign army; his only thought is capitulation. The chapter is also setting up the LORD's coming intervention. The prophet of v13 will appear precisely because Ahab is spiritually bankrupt. The LORD will demonstrate his sovereignty over Aram not because Israel deserves it but because the LORD has a name to vindicate even among an apostate king's reign.

And the messengers came again, and said, Thus speaketh Benhadad, saying, Although I have sent unto thee, saying, Thou shalt deliver me thy silver, and thy gold, and thy wives, and thy children; Yet I will send my servants unto thee to morrow about this time, and they shall search thine house, and the houses of thy servants; and it shall be, that whatsoever is pleasant in thine eyes, they shall put it in their hand, and take it away. Then the king of Israel called all the elders of the land, and said, Mark, I pray you, and see how this man seeketh mischief: for he sent unto me for my wives, and for my children, and for my silver, and for my gold; and I denied him not. And all the elders and all the people said unto him, Hearken not unto him, nor consent. Wherefore he said unto the messengers of Benhadad, Tell my lord the king, All that thou didst send for to thy servant at the first I will do: but this thing I may not do. And the messengers departed, and brought him word again. Guidance: The escalated demand. Ben-hadad, having received Ahab's surrender, escalates the terms. He is not satisfied with the king's silver, gold, wives, and children; he now demands the right to send his soldiers door-to-door through Samaria — not just the palace but 'the houses of thy servants' — to take anything they want. This is plunder under the cover of vassalage; an Aramean military expedition through the streets of Samaria, taking whatever pleased them. The new demand crosses a line Ahab cannot cross. To accept it would mean handing over not just his own household but every Israelite household to looting. The king, finally, consults his elders. They speak unanimously: 'Hearken not unto him, nor consent.' The chapter is showing us a leader belatedly turning to wise counsel. Ahab had surrendered personally without consultation; he summons the elders only when his subjects' homes are threatened, not when only his own family was at risk. His character is laid bare: he could surrender his own family but not the people's property. (Whether for nobler reasons or because the political cost of looting was higher than the personal cost, the text leaves us to judge.) Ahab sends back the partial answer: I will give up everything I personally agreed to in the first message; the new demand I cannot accept. The chapter is teaching us that there is sometimes a moral floor below which even weak leaders refuse to go. Ahab is far from a model king, but in this moment he draws a line. The line is drawn too late and at the wrong place, but it is drawn.

And Benhadad sent unto him, and said, The gods do so unto me, and more also, if the dust of Samaria shall suffice for handfuls for all the people that follow me. And the king of Israel answered and said, Tell him, Let not him that girdeth on his harness boast himself as he that putteth it off. And it came to pass, when Benhadad heard this message, as he was drinking, he and the kings in the pavilions, that he said unto his servants, Set yourselves in array. And they set themselves in array against the city. Guidance: The exchange of taunts. Ben-hadad escalates with a contemptuous boast: 'The gods do so unto me, and more also, if the dust of Samaria shall suffice for handfuls for all the people that follow me.' The boast is hyperbolic — his army is so numerous that, when they reduce Samaria to rubble, the dust will not even be enough to fill each soldier's cupped hand as a souvenir. Ahab's reply is a famous proverb: 'Let not him that girdeth on his harness boast himself as he that putteth it off.' Hebrew al-yithallel chogger ki-mephatteah — 'let not the one girding boast like the one ungirding.' The image: do not celebrate the battle before it is fought. The man putting on his armor (preparing for war) should not speak with the confidence of the man removing his armor (returning victorious). The proverb has become idiomatic in many languages — 'don't count your chickens before they hatch,' 'don't celebrate the win before the game's over.' Ahab speaks it well; he has lived long enough at court to know military bluster from military success. Ben-hadad hears the reply 'as he was drinking, he and the kings in the pavilions.' The Aramean king and his 32 vassal-allies are mid-banquet, well into their cups, when Ahab's message arrives. Drunkenness and overconfidence combine in Ben-hadad's response: 'Set yourselves in array' — prepare for the assault. He has been challenged; he will not back down. The chapter is preparing us for what comes. A foreign army drunk and overconfident; a beleaguered city defended by a king who has just demonstrated reluctance to fight; the LORD waiting to demonstrate that Aramean military might is no match for His decision. The contrast — drinking pavilion vs. besieged city — is itself the LORD's setup. The mighty have made themselves vulnerable through arrogance and intoxication; the weak are about to win because the LORD has chosen so.

And, behold, there came a prophet unto Ahab king of Israel, saying, Thus saith the LORD, Hast thou seen all this great multitude? behold, I will deliver it into thine hand this day; and thou shalt know that I am the LORD. And Ahab said, By whom? And he said, Thus saith the LORD, Even by the young men of the princes of the provinces. Then he said, Who shall order the battle? And he answered, Thou. Then he numbered the young men of the princes of the provinces, and they were two hundred and thirty two: and after them he numbered all the people, even all the children of Israel, being seven thousand. Guidance: The unnamed prophet's promise. The chapter introduces an unnamed prophet — not Elijah, who is presumably still at Horeb or with Elisha. The northern kingdom had multiple prophetic ministries; Obadiah had hidden 100; the 7000 of 19:18 included other devout men. This is one of them. He brings the LORD's promise: 'Hast thou seen all this great multitude? behold, I will deliver it into thine hand this day; and thou shalt know that I am the LORD.' The purpose-clause is striking: 'thou shalt know that I am the LORD.' The LORD is intervening not because Ahab is faithful but because Ahab needs to know. Even the most apostate king will be confronted with concrete evidence of who controls history. Ahab asks two practical questions. (1) 'By whom?' (2) 'Who shall order the battle?' The answers: 'By the young men of the princes of the provinces' (na'arei sarei ham-medinot — the young attendants of the provincial governors, perhaps the equivalent of cadets or junior officers); the king himself will lead. Ahab counts them: 232 young men. Then 'all the children of Israel' — the entire mobilizable army in Samaria — only 7,000 men. The chapter is making the numerical asymmetry shocking. Ben-hadad has at least 32 kings, with their combined armies — tens of thousands of men. Israel has 7,000. The shock-force will be only 232. The LORD is deliberately setting up impossible odds. The chapter is showing us a recurring biblical pattern: the LORD prefers victories that cannot be explained by human strength. Gideon's 300 vs. Midian's hordes (Judges 7); David vs. Goliath; the early disciples vs. Rome. The LORD's victories are designed to leave no doubt about their source. The 232-against-Ben-hadad's-army setup is another instance of the principle. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's interventions often involve disproportionate scales — His agents are few, the opposition large; the victory is unmistakably divine.

And, behold, there came a prophet unto Ahab king of Israel, saying, Thus saith the LORD, Hast thou seen all this great multitude? behold, I will deliver it into thine hand this day; and thou shalt know that I am the LORD. And Ahab said, By whom? And he said, Thus saith the LORD, Even by the young men of the princes of the provinces. Then he said, Who shall order the battle? And he answered, Thou. Then he numbered the young men of the princes of the provinces, and they were two hundred and thirty two: and after them he numbered all the people, even all the children of Israel, being seven thousand. Guidance: The unnamed prophet's promise. The chapter introduces an unnamed prophet — not Elijah, who is presumably still at Horeb or with Elisha. The northern kingdom had multiple prophetic ministries; Obadiah had hidden 100; the 7000 of 19:18 included other devout men. This is one of them. He brings the LORD's promise: 'Hast thou seen all this great multitude? behold, I will deliver it into thine hand this day; and thou shalt know that I am the LORD.' The purpose-clause is striking: 'thou shalt know that I am the LORD.' The LORD is intervening not because Ahab is faithful but because Ahab needs to know. Even the most apostate king will be confronted with concrete evidence of who controls history. Ahab asks two practical questions. (1) 'By whom?' (2) 'Who shall order the battle?' The answers: 'By the young men of the princes of the provinces' (na'arei sarei ham-medinot — the young attendants of the provincial governors, perhaps the equivalent of cadets or junior officers); the king himself will lead. Ahab counts them: 232 young men. Then 'all the children of Israel' — the entire mobilizable army in Samaria — only 7,000 men. The chapter is making the numerical asymmetry shocking. Ben-hadad has at least 32 kings, with their combined armies — tens of thousands of men. Israel has 7,000. The shock-force will be only 232. The LORD is deliberately setting up impossible odds. The chapter is showing us a recurring biblical pattern: the LORD prefers victories that cannot be explained by human strength. Gideon's 300 vs. Midian's hordes (Judges 7); David vs. Goliath; the early disciples vs. Rome. The LORD's victories are designed to leave no doubt about their source. The 232-against-Ben-hadad's-army setup is another instance of the principle. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's interventions often involve disproportionate scales — His agents are few, the opposition large; the victory is unmistakably divine.

And they went out at noon. But Benhadad was drinking himself drunk in the pavilions, he and the kings, the thirty and two kings that helped him. And the young men of the princes of the provinces went out first; and Benhadad sent out, and they told him, saying, There are men come out of Samaria. And he said, Whether they be come out for peace, take them alive; or whether they be come out for war, take them alive. So these young men of the princes of the provinces came out of the city, and the army which followed them. And they slew every one his man: and the Syrians fled; and Israel pursued them: and Benhadad the king of Syria escaped on an horse with the horsemen. And the king of Israel went out, and smote the horses and chariots, and slew the Syrians with a great slaughter. Guidance: The noon attack. Israel sortied at noon — the heat of midday, when armies typically rested. Ben-hadad, drunk in his tent with the 32 kings, was completely unprepared. 'There are men come out of Samaria.' His response, slurred and overconfident: 'Whether they be come out for peace, take them alive; or whether they be come out for war, take them alive.' He cannot conceive that 232 men leaving the besieged city are an attack force. He instructs his soldiers to capture them, not kill them, regardless of intent. The miscalculation is fatal. The 232 cadets emerge, followed by the 7,000 Israelite army. 'They slew every one his man.' The Arameans, caught off-guard by an organized military strike when they expected hostages-coming-to-surrender, panic and flee. Ben-hadad himself escapes on horseback, leaving his army behind. Ahab pursues, capturing horses and chariots, slaughtering Aramean forces. 'The Syrians with a great slaughter.' The chapter is showing us how the LORD's promise materialized. The 232 cadets were not, militarily speaking, a sufficient strike-force. But the conditions the LORD arranged — drunk command, complacent expectation, the noon timing — multiplied their effectiveness many times over. The Aramean army's strength was real but irrelevant; the campaign was lost before it was fully engaged because the leadership was incapacitated. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's victories often come through unexpected timing and weakness in the opponent's command structure. He does not always defeat enemies by overwhelming them; sometimes He defeats them by exposing their pre-existing self-destruction. Ben-hadad and his kings drank themselves into vulnerability; the LORD let the Israelite cadets walk through the open door.

And the prophet came to the king of Israel, and said unto him, Go, strengthen thyself, and mark, and see what thou doest: for at the return of the year the king of Syria will come up against thee. And the servants of the king of Syria said unto him, Their gods are gods of the hills; therefore they were stronger than we; but let us fight against them in the plain, and surely we shall be stronger than they: And do this thing, Take the kings away, every man out of his place, and put captains in their rooms: And number thee an army, like the army that thou hast lost, horse for horse, and chariot for chariot: and we will fight against them in the plain, and surely we shall be stronger than they. And he hearkened unto their voice, and did so. Guidance: The Aramean reassessment. Two parallel councils after the first defeat. (1) The prophet returns to Ahab: 'Go, strengthen thyself, and mark, and see what thou doest: for at the return of the year the king of Syria will come up against thee.' The annual military-campaign season was spring, after the winter rains. The prophet warns Ahab that Aram will be back, gives him a year to prepare. (2) Ben-hadad's advisors offer a diagnosis: 'Their gods are gods of the hills; therefore they were stronger than we; but let us fight against them in the plain, and surely we shall be stronger than they.' The Arameans' theological analysis is laughably wrong. They believe the LORD is a local mountain-deity who is only powerful on hilly terrain (Samaria sits on its hill); on the plains the Israelite god supposedly cannot operate. The misunderstanding reflects the standard ancient-Near-East polytheistic worldview — gods had territorial domains, and different gods controlled different geographic regions. The LORD of Israel will, in the next paragraph, demonstrate the catastrophic error of this analysis. The advisors' practical recommendation: (a) replace the 32 vassal-kings with professional military captains (the kings, presumably the source of the drinking-pavilion problem at the first campaign, are removed from field command); (b) rebuild the army to its previous numbers. Ben-hadad accepts. The chapter is showing us a defeated empire's failed reform. The Arameans correctly diagnose part of their problem (the kings' incompetence) but miss the deeper issue (they were fighting the LORD). They restructure the human aspects while leaving the underlying theological miscalculation intact. The chapter is teaching us that defeats by the LORD's hand cannot be reversed by tactical improvements. If the basic enemy is divine, no human strategy adjustment will alter the outcome. The Arameans will discover this at Aphek.

And it came to pass at the return of the year, that Benhadad numbered the Syrians, and went up to Aphek, to fight against Israel. And the children of Israel were numbered, and were all present, and went against them: and the children of Israel pitched before them like two little flocks of kids; but the Syrians filled the country. And there came a man of God, and spake unto the king of Israel, and said, Thus saith the LORD, Because the Syrians have said, The LORD is God of the hills, but he is not God of the valleys, therefore will I deliver all this great multitude into thine hand, and ye shall know that I am the LORD. And they pitched one over against the other seven days. And so it was, that in the seventh day the battle was joined: and the children of Israel slew of the Syrians an hundred thousand footmen in one day. But the rest fled to Aphek, into the city; and there a wall fell upon twenty and seven thousand of the men that were left. And Benhadad fled, and came into the city, into an inner chamber. Guidance: The Aphek battle. Spring comes; Ben-hadad mobilizes the rebuilt army and marches to Aphek (a town east of the Sea of Galilee, on the plains the Arameans believed would favor them). Israel mobilizes too. The visual is striking: 'The children of Israel pitched before them like two little flocks of kids' — two small groups of goat-kids huddled together — 'but the Syrians filled the country.' The numerical imbalance is overwhelming. The Aramean army fills the plain; the Israelites are two small clusters. Then the man of God appears: 'Thus saith the LORD, Because the Syrians have said, The LORD is God of the hills, but he is not God of the valleys, therefore will I deliver all this great multitude into thine hand, and ye shall know that I am the LORD.' The LORD is responding to the Aramean theological insult. They claimed He was a regional mountain-god; He will now prove He is the God of all the earth. The seven-day standoff is recorded — the armies face each other without engaging for a week. Then on the seventh day, battle. The result: 100,000 Aramean foot-soldiers killed in a single day. (The number is large but not impossibly so by ancient near-east standards.) The survivors flee to Aphek's city walls — and a wall (presumably the defensive wall of the city itself or one near it) collapses on another 27,000 men. 127,000 Aramean casualties from a single day's fighting. The chapter is showing us divine victory at scale. The LORD's response to a theological insult is not a corrective lecture but a battlefield demonstration. The Arameans claimed He was confined to the hills; He destroyed them on the plain. The pattern echoes Pharaoh in Exodus: those who deny the LORD's universal sovereignty discover it through catastrophe. Ben-hadad flees into Aphek and hides in 'an inner chamber.' The king of the great Aramean empire is reduced to a fugitive in a back room. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's victories are also vindications of His character. The Arameans had spoken evil of His name; the LORD answered the slander with a demonstration that left no surviving witnesses able to repeat the slander. The God of the valleys is, in fact, the God of all places.

And it came to pass at the return of the year, that Benhadad numbered the Syrians, and went up to Aphek, to fight against Israel. And the children of Israel were numbered, and were all present, and went against them: and the children of Israel pitched before them like two little flocks of kids; but the Syrians filled the country. And there came a man of God, and spake unto the king of Israel, and said, Thus saith the LORD, Because the Syrians have said, The LORD is God of the hills, but he is not God of the valleys, therefore will I deliver all this great multitude into thine hand, and ye shall know that I am the LORD. And they pitched one over against the other seven days. And so it was, that in the seventh day the battle was joined: and the children of Israel slew of the Syrians an hundred thousand footmen in one day. And his servants said unto him, Behold now, we have heard that the kings of the house of Israel are merciful kings: let us, I pray thee, put sackcloth on our loins, and ropes upon our heads, and go out to the king of Israel: peradventure he will save thy life. So they girded sackcloth on their loins, and put ropes on their heads, and came to the king of Israel, and said, Thy servant Benhadad saith, I pray thee, let me live. And he said, Is he yet alive? he is my brother. Now the men did diligently observe whether any thing would come from him, and did hastily catch it: and they said, Thy brother Benhadad. Then he said, Go ye, bring him. Then Benhadad came forth to him; and he caused him to come up into the chariot. And Benhadad said unto him, The cities, which my father took from thy father, I will restore; and thou shalt make streets for thee in Damascus, as my father made in Samaria. Then said Ahab, I will send thee away with this covenant. So he made a covenant with him, and sent him away. Guidance: Ahab's foolish mercy. The Aramean staff strategizes: 'The kings of the house of Israel are merciful kings.' (The Israelite kings have a regional reputation for mercy in surrender — likely from observed historical patterns. The Arameans calculate the right approach.) They send Ben-hadad to Ahab as a humble penitent — sackcloth, ropes around their heads (signs of complete submission) — asking only for his life. Ahab's response is shocking: 'Is he yet alive? he is my brother.' The Hebrew achi hu — 'he is my brother.' Ahab calls the man who had besieged Samaria and demanded his wives and children 'my brother.' The Aramean envoys 'did diligently observe whether any thing would come from him, and did hastily catch it: and they said, Thy brother Benhadad.' They seize the favorable word ('brother') and run with it — confirming the relationship. Ahab calls Ben-hadad up into his chariot — the supreme honor of receiving him as an equal-king. They negotiate terms: restoration of cities his father (Ben-hadad I) had taken; commercial privileges in Damascus. Ahab signs a covenant and sends Ben-hadad home. The chapter is recording an act of catastrophic political mercy. Ben-hadad — the man who had besieged Samaria, threatened to plunder it, lost 127,000 men trying to take it — walks away under treaty. The LORD's clear victory becomes Ahab's diplomatic settlement. The chapter is preparing us for the prophetic verdict that follows. Ben-hadad will be back; the cities will eventually be lost again; the Aramean threat will continue to haunt Israel for generations. Ahab's mercy looked statesmanlike; it actually preserved the very enemy the LORD had defeated. The chapter is teaching us a hard lesson about mercy out of place. Mercy is the LORD's character; misapplied mercy frustrates His judgment. Ahab thought he was being generous; he was undoing the LORD's deliverance.

And a certain man of the sons of the prophets said unto his neighbour in the word of the LORD, Smite me, I pray thee. And the man refused to smite him. Then said he unto him, Because thou hast not obeyed the voice of the LORD, behold, as soon as thou art departed from me, a lion shall slay thee. And as soon as he was departed from him, a lion found him, and slew him. Then he found another man, and said, Smite me, I pray thee. And the man smote him, so that in smiting he wounded him. So the prophet departed, and waited for the king by the way, and disguised himself with ashes upon his face. Guidance: The prophetic sign-act prepared. The chapter introduces a 'man of the sons of the prophets' — a member of the prophetic guild (perhaps connected to those Obadiah had hidden in caves; the prophetic community in the north was small but real). He needs a visible wound to deliver his coming parable to Ahab. He asks a colleague: 'Smite me, I pray thee.' The colleague refuses — strikingly, despite the instruction being 'in the word of the LORD.' The prophet pronounces judgment: 'Because thou hast not obeyed the voice of the LORD, behold, as soon as thou art departed from me, a lion shall slay thee.' The colleague departs and a lion kills him. The chapter is preserving a sobering vignette. The colleague's refusal is morally understandable (who wants to hit a friend?), but the LORD's instruction was clear, and the colleague's refusal violated it. The lion-execution echoes the lion of 1 Kgs 13:24 — the LORD uses lions occasionally as instruments of judgment on disobedient servants. The prophet finds another man, who does strike him, wounding him visibly. Now the prophet has the visible injury needed for his parable. He waits by the road, disguising himself further with ashes on his face. The chapter is showing us prophetic theater being prepared. The wound is not for healing; it is for messaging. The prophet will use his own injury as the stage prop for confronting Ahab. The chapter is teaching us that prophetic ministry sometimes requires physical investment. The LORD's prophets do not always speak from comfortable distances; they sometimes inflict the message on their own bodies to make the truth visible. (Isaiah will walk naked for three years; Jeremiah will wear a wooden yoke; Ezekiel will lie on his side for over a year. The pattern is established here.)

And as the king passed by, he cried unto the king: and he said, Thy servant went out into the midst of the battle; and, behold, a man turned aside, and brought a man unto me, and said, Keep this man: if by any means he be missing, then shall thy life be for his life, or else thou shalt pay a talent of silver. And as thy servant was busy here and there, he was gone. And the king of Israel said unto him, So shall thy judgment be; thyself hast decided it. And he hasted, and took the ashes away from his face; and the king of Israel discerned him that he was of the prophets. And he said unto him, Thus saith the LORD, Because thou hast let go out of thy hand a man whom I appointed to utter destruction, therefore thy life shall go for his life, and thy people for his people. And the king of Israel went to his house heavy and displeased, and came to Samaria. Guidance: The parable confronts Ahab. The prophet, disguised, calls out to the king as he passes by. He tells the made-up story of a soldier given a prisoner with strict instructions: 'Keep this man: if by any means he be missing, then shall thy life be for his life, or else thou shalt pay a talent of silver.' The soldier got distracted; the prisoner escaped. Ahab issues the verdict without thinking: 'So shall thy judgment be; thyself hast decided it.' The king is binding the case by the soldier's own admission — the deal he had agreed to is the deal that applies. The prophet then removes his disguise. Ahab recognizes him as a prophet — the disguise had been to keep Ahab from recognizing him until the verdict was given. The trap snaps shut. 'Thus saith the LORD, Because thou hast let go out of thy hand a man whom I appointed to utter destruction, therefore thy life shall go for his life, and thy people for his people.' The Hebrew ish chermi — 'a man of my destruction-ban.' Ben-hadad had been under the divine cherem (the dedicated-for-destruction ban that meant the LORD had marked him for elimination). By letting him go, Ahab has substituted himself for Ben-hadad. Ahab's life will go for Ben-hadad's life; Ahab's people will go for Ben-hadad's people. The parallel to Nathan's confrontation of David (2 Sam 12) is exact in structure. The prophet tells a story; the king pronounces judgment; the prophet reveals that the story is about the king himself. Both Nathan and this prophet use the same trap. 'And the king of Israel went to his house heavy and displeased, and came to Samaria.' Hebrew sar va-za'ef — 'sullen and angry.' Ahab does not repent. He is heavy-hearted (sar — sullen, depressed) and angry (za'ef — irritated, vexed) at receiving the prophetic indictment. He does not change his mind about Ben-hadad; he does not seek to undo the covenant; he simply broods. The chapter is ending with the king's resentful silence. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD holds even apostate kings to His covenant standards. Ahab's foolish mercy on Ben-hadad has consequences that will ultimately cost him. The chapter is preparing us for the conflict ahead — Ahab's discontent with the LORD's verdict on his treaty will fuel his next moral failures (the Naboth-vineyard episode in chapter 21, the death-prophecy at Ramoth-gilead in chapter 22). The seeds of his doom are being planted by his own choices in this chapter. The chapter is showing us a king who refuses to learn from the LORD's mercy or to bow to the LORD's verdict — and whose stubbornness will produce his own ruin.
